steps.title: Restate the conclusion
steps.content: Whiteflies induce tomato plants to emit chemicals that make other tomato plants preferentially mount the microbial defense (salicylic acid) instead of the herbivory defense (jasmonic acid), even when those other plants are then attacked by whiteflies (herbivory).

steps.title: What data would support that
steps.content: Plants exposed to air from whitefly-infested plants, then infested themselves, should produce less jasmonic acid and more salicylic acid than controls. Plants exposed to air from whitefly-free plants should show the normal herbivory response (jasmonic acid rises) when infested.

steps.title: Eliminate per choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'air from whitefly-free plants...more jasmonic acid than...controls; air from whitefly-infested plants...less jasmonic acid and more salicylic acid than...controls': matches both halves — the infested-air group switches toward the microbial defense, the free-air group mounts the expected herbivory defense. ✓
  • Choice B — 'less jasmonic acid than salicylic acid...about the same amount of jasmonic acid and salicylic acid': compares the two acids within each group instead of comparing each group to the control, so it cannot show that the infested-air signal caused a shift (misread of what supports the conclusion). ✗
  • Choice C — 'exclusively produced salicylic acid': overstates the result — the conclusion is preferential defense, not exclusive production of salicylic acid (overreach). ✗
  • Choice D — 'air from whitefly-free plants...more jasmonic acid and salicylic acid than...controls': reverses the expected free-air baseline and conflates the two defenses, so it does not show a preferential switch to microbial defense in the infested-air group (misread of the data pattern needed). ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
